It's possible to sort strings in AWK, as well as uniq-ing, meaning you can replace uniq(1) and sort(1) calls with just the one call of AWK. Granted, you can use `sort -u` to do both, but AWK offers much more functionality. Unlike when using AWK to uniq-ify, uniq(1) only works by adjacency, meaning the duplicate lines must be adjacent to one another for them to be handled.

examples.description: Expand a regular variable.

Some people like to cuddle the variable name with braces ('{' and '}') but this is usually superfluous and wasted keystrokes, unless you need to protect the variable name from having other characters included, as in the below example, or you're using one of the many features of parameter expansion.

Access a given index in an array. In this example, you don't technically need to specify the element, because by default the first element is used. As with many other languages, note that indices are 0-first, so 1 is the 2nd.
You can use arithmetic between '[' and ']' as well.
Expand variable to the length of that to which the variable would expand.
Expand array variable to the number of elements/indices. You may find that `[*]` works as well as `[@]`, in this case.
Expand variable to a substring. In this case, imagine `Var` is equal to the string 'thing', but the offset is 2 and the length is 1, giving us an 'i'.

Expand variable to a substring by removing the matched glob pattern from left to right. To make this global (IE: greedy) use two '#' characters. So, in this example, everything, from left to right, up to and including a 'T' or 't', will be removed, but it would only happen once.
As above, but from right to left. Use two '%' characters for a greedy match.
Change how the variable expands by using pattern substitution. This uses glob pattern matching, not REGEX. If the first '/' is instead '//', a greedy match is performed.

A good example of the above, which will list directories in PATH which exist and are directories. It works because all instances of ':' are replaced with a whitespace, causing find(1) to see multiple directories (fields) in which to search.
Expand the variable to the string between `:-` and the closing `}`, if that variable doesn't already have a value. This can be useful to set a default.
This is a way of displaying an error message if the contents of the variable is empty. It will also immediately exit with an exit status of 1.
Indirect expansion exists in a couple of ways in BASH. If `Var` is equal to `OtherVar`, and that `OtherVar` is equal to `true`, the below example would expand to `true`.
Expand variable so that the first letter is uppercase. Use two '^' (carets) if you want the entire contents of the variable to change to uppercase.
As above, but convert to lowercase. Use two ',' characters to transform the entire string to which `Var` expands.

With 'root' privileges, use du(1), sort(1), and head(1) to display a list of the top 20 space-consuming files in whichever storage medium '/' is mounted. Here, du(1) is using the `-x` flag to keep to the one filesystem, which is important for getting accurate results on the filesystem on which you might, for example, be needing to free space. In order to sort the human-readable file sizes, sort(1) is using the `-h` flag, the `-k` flag to specify the column to sort (first), and its using the `-r` flag to reverse the sorting, so we see the highest size first. To then show the top-20 lines, we use head(1) and specify the number of lines via the `-n` flag. The default number of lines displayed by head(1) and tail(1) is 10. Root privileges are gained for this task by using sudo(8) on bash(1) in order to have a new root-owned BASH session, which then executes the commands proceeding the `-c` flag.
Display just the total human-readable size of the current working directory.
Display the total human-readable size of the three provided directories, as well as the grand total of the combined directories.
You could potentially make this task a bit easier with BASH brace expansion.

Find files by case-insensitive extension, such as `.jpg`, `.JPG`, & `.jpG`). By default, find(1) uses glob pathname pattern matching. To avoid shell interpretation, the glob either must be expanded or the string quoted. Period is optional; it's implied unless a path is provided. find(1) works recursively unless otherwise directed (IE: `-maxdepth [N]`).
Find directories.
Find files. Specifically files; not directories, links, FIFOs, etc.
Find files set to the provided octal mode (permissions).
Find files with setuid bit set, keeping to the same filesystem.

The above is a useful demonstration of some pitfalls into which a user can fall, where the below is the above but corrected. Here is why: * The `.` (current working directory) is assumed when no path is provided. * Group syntax (parentheses) was used, but nothing was actually grouped. * A lot of people have their ls(1) command aliased in many ways, - potentially causing problems with the output and how xargs(1) handles it. By escaping the command, we temporarily override any aliases and even functions by the same name. * At least in my experience, the prior xargs(1) is not as reliable. * The `-print0` and `xargs -0` is great, but unnecessary (except when?). However, it might be more preferred to simply use find(1)'s own `-printf` flag, in order to avoid the need for xargs(1) and ls(1), which should be many times faster, and allows for more specificity.
Find and remove files with case-sensitive extension of `.txt`.

The above is much more efficiently written as shown below, as find(1) has its own built-in delete function, not to mention a single rm(1) process was previously executed for each file processed, which is comparatively slow.
Find files with extension '.txt' and look for a string into them.
Find files with size bigger than 5 Mb and sort them by size.
Find files bigger thank 2 MB and list them.

Alternative, faster approach* to the above. Why it's faster: * No need for an external process, like ls(1). * The use of `;` with the `-exec` flag executes an ls(1) process for each file found, which is comparatively very slow. * The `printf` feature is built in and special to awk(1). That said, awk(1) or gawk(1) is doing a little more here, in order to get somewhat of a human-readable file size, but its impact is likely negligible.

A far more efficient approach to the above. If no path is provided, then the current working directory (CWD) is assumed, making the `.` superfluous.
Search for directories named `build` at a maximum depth of 2 directories. This means that find will not recursively search beyond two levels.

Search all files which are not in a `.git` directory. Depending on the shell used, the bang (`!`) may need to be escaped, to avoid shell interpretation. Alternatively, although non-POSIX, the `-not` flag can be used.

Find all files that have the same inode (indicating hard link) as FILE. All output going to STDERR (typically error messages) will also be redirected to `/dev/null`, a special pseudo-file where data is sent to die.
Find all files in the current directory and modify their permissions.

Find files with extension `.txt` and edit all of them with vim(1). The use of `+` (escaped to avoid shell interpretation) with `-exec` means that only one process (in this case, `vim`) per `exec`ution is used. If `;` is instead used (would also need escaping), then one `vim` process would be used per file.

Find files with extension `.png`, then rename their extension to `.jpg`. It's highly important that `\;` is used here, instead of `\+`, otherwise it'd make a right mess of the files, due to the way in which mv(1) works.

The GNU sort(1) command can also filter out adjacent duplicate lines and can therefore overlap with the uniq(1) command. However, uniq(1) has some options that sort(1) cannot do so refer to the man page for you situation if you require something beyond a basic uniqueness check. In addition, there is the potential for parallizing the processing by piping sort(1) into uniq(1) for non trivial tasks. By default, sort(1) sorts lines or fields using the ASCII table. Here, we're essentially getting alphanumeric sorting, where case is handled separately; - this results in these words being adjacent to one another, thus duplicates are removed. If you need better uniq-ing, you could refer to AWK & its associative arrays.
examples.command: printf '%s\n' this is a list of of random words with duplicate words | sort -u

examples.description: Sort numerically. If you don't provide the `-n` flag, sort(1) will instead sort by the ASCII table, as mentioned above, meaning it'll display as 1, 10, - 11, 2, 3, 4, etc.

You can even sort human-readable sizes. In this example, the 2nd column is being sorted, thanks to the use of the `-k` flag, and the sorting is reversed, so that the top-most storage space hungry filesystems are displayed from df(1).
SSH in via PEM file, which normally needs 0600 permissions.
Connect through a non-standard port. It's recommended not to use the default port of 22, as it is so often targeted, due to it being so commonplace.
Connect and forward the authentication agent.
Execute a command on a remote server.
Tunnel an X session over SSH, via X11 Forwarding.
Redirect traffic with a tunnel between local host (port 8080) and a remote host (remote.example.com:5000) through a proxy (personal.server.com).
Launch a specific X application over SSH.
Create a SOCKS proxy on localhost and port 9999.

Connect to server, but allow for X11 forwarding, while also using Gzip compression (can be much faster; YMMV), and using the `blowfish` encryption. For more information, see: http://unix.stackexchange.com/q/12755/44856

Copy files and directories, via SSH, from remote host to the current working directory, with Gzip compression. An option for when `rsync` isn't available. This works by creating (not temporary!) a remote Tar archive, then piping its output to a local Tar process, which then extracts it to STDOUT.
examples.command: ssh user@example.com 'tar -C /var/www/Shared/ zcf - asset1 asset2' | tar zxf -

examples.description: Explicitly specify a key for connection. Useful if you have too many authentication failures for a given username.
Temporarily disable `pubkey` authentication for this instance.
Mount a remote directory or filesystem, through SSH, to a local mount point. Install SSHFS from: https://github.com/libfuse/sshfs
EMACS can read files through SSH. Below, is a link to related documentation. http://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/emacs/Remote-Files.html
Get help for SSH escape sequences. Useful for terminating unresponsive sessions. The default escape character is ~ (tilde), escapes are only recognized immediately after a newline.
display numerical values for file permissions
Display only the octal permissions for the given directory. Great for tests.

Show only a given value from one of the `show` keys. In this example, the value for the `ActiveState` key for the UFW service will be shown, and only it; ideal for scripting. Using the `--value` flag causes only the value to be displayed.
Start, stop, or restart a given service(s).

Check if a given service(s) is active. If it is, 'active' will display. An exit status of 0 will be given if it's active, and non-zero otherwise. Use the `-q` or `--quiet` flag to rely only on the exit status.

Check if a given service(s) has failed. If it is, 'failed' will display. An exit status of 0 will be given if it has failed, and non-zero otherwise. Use the `-q` or `--quiet` flag to rely only on the exit status.

Check if a given service(s) is enabled. If it is, 'enabled' will display. An exit status of 0 will be given if it's enabled, and non-zero otherwise. Use the `-q` or `--quiet` flag to rely only on the exit status.
List all failed services.
Shut the system down. Use `suspend` to suspend, `halt` to halt, and `reboot` to instead of reboot the machine.
Enable or disable a given service(s).
Show the current status of a given service(s).

An approach to backing up the current user's HOME, using tar(1) and Gzip compression. Permissions (modes) will be preserved. The filename format will be: UID:GID_DATE.tgz Replace 'DEVICE' with whichever device is applicable to you, but note that it must be in the '/media/USER' (where USER is the username) directory, else this won't work, unless you edit the formatting section of `printf`.

Delete file 'xdm' from the archive given to the `-f` flag. This only works on non-compressed archives, unfortunately, but those can always be uncompressed first, then altered with the `--delete` flag, after which you can recompress.

Extract the contents of the given archive (which is not compressed) to the destination given to the `-C` flag; not many seem to know of this flag. If a destination (path given to `-C`) is not provided, the CWD will be used.
Capture packets from a specific network interface
Capture only a certain number of packets
Capture and write packets to a file for later analysis
Read packets from a file
Capture packets from a specific host
Capture packets from a specific port
Capture packets based on a specific protocol (e.g., TCP)
Capture packets and filter for HTTP traffic
Capture packets from a specific source IP
Capture packets to a specific destination IP
Display captured packets with timestamp
Capture packets and display with verbose output
Capture packets and display with extra verbose output
Capture packets and resolve hostnames
Capture packets and disable resolving hostnames
Capture packets and show link-layer headers
Capture IPv6 packets
Capture packets larger than a specific size
Display `ls` output to the user, but also write it to the given file.
As above, but append the data; previous file's data remains intact while new data is added at the end of the file.
Pipe the standard output of a given command into `tee`, which then displays it to the user and sending the data to files `one`, `two`, and `three`.
Workaround to output data to a file, with root privileges.
Pipe the current Vim buffer to a shell process, which in this case is `tee`. This is especially useful as a shortcut added to `.vimrc` or similar.
Connect to a remote host on a specified port
Open a telnet session without specifying a port (default to port 23)
Open a telnet session with option for terminal type
Interrupt a running command or return to the telnet prompt
examples.command: Ctrl + ] (Escape sequence, type this combo while in a telnet session)

examples.description: Display telnet’s internal help
Telnet quit command to exit the session
examples.command: quit (Type this command within the telnet session to exit)

examples.description: Set or show telnet options
examples.command: telnet set option (Enter this within the telnet command line to configure options)

examples.description: Send special telnet sequence to the host
examples.command: send set_sequence_name (Enter this within a telnet session to send specific sequences such as break)

examples.description: Start a new session:
Start a new named session:
List existing sessions:
Attach to the most recently used session:
Attach to a named session:
Basic usage to display the route taken by packets to reach a host
Specify the maximum number of hops (TTL) to be used
Use a particular protocol (ICMP or UDP)
cheat.sheets: traceroute
Specify the number of probe packets per hop
Set the initial TTL (Time-To-Live)
Specify the wait time for a response
Change the default port
Specify the interface to be used
Display the IP addresses numerically without resolving hostnames
Set the source address
Download a file from a given URL and save it in the current directory
Download a file and save it with a different name
Download files in the background
Download a file and limit the download speed to reduce bandwidth usage
Download files from a list of URLs provided in a text file
Resume an incomplete download
Download a file while showing the progress in a more readable form
Download a file without checking the server's SSL certificate
Download an entire website for offline browsing
Download a file with HTTP authentication
Download a file with cookies, useful for sites that require logins
Download a directory from an FTP server
Download only files newer than the local version
Download a file only if it has been updated on the server
Quietly download a file, continuing where it left of, if the connection fails. The file will be downloaded to the current working directory.
Specify a location to download the given file.
Download URL using the user agent string provided to the `-U` flag.
Check domain registration information
Retrieve registration details of an IP address
Query a specific WHOIS server for domain information
Display command usage information in case of errors or unsure use
Use specific WHOIS server on a non-standard port
Format output to be more readable by human
Display help and additional options
Find all file names ending with .pdf, then remove them.
The above, however, is better-written without xargs:
Although it's best to use find's own functionality, in this situation.

Find all file names ending with '.pdf' and remove them. This approach also handles filenames with '\n' and skips '*.pdf' directories. The xargs(1) flag `-r` is equivalent to `--no-run-if-empty`, and the use of `-n` will in this case group execution by 10 files.

If file names may contains spaces, you can use the xargs(1) flag `-I` and its proceeding argument to specify the filename placeholder, as in find(1)'s use of `{}` in `-exec`. Although find(1)'s `{}` needs not be cuddled by quotes, - xargs(1) does.
Print a list of files in the format of `*FILE=`. The use of xargs(1) flag `-n` here with its argument of `1` means to process the files one-by-one.
The above is, however, much faster, more efficient, and easier without xargs.
Group words by three in a string.
Alternatively, and more efficiently, use Bash brace expansion, if available.
